description: Grouping bounds *teardown*, not visibility (#lzspecedgeindex). A group's nodes read parent-owned and sibling-group-owned nodes freely, and grouping never restricts what an edge may point at. A binding that treats `child()` as a scope in the lexical or ownership sense — refusing or erroring on a cross-group read, or silently returning a stale value for one — is non-conforming, and would break the primary use of groups: a per-connection or per-subscription group whose nodes derive from long-lived application state that outlives every group. The fixture reads in all three directions (child reads parent, child reads sibling's node, parent reads child's node) and asserts each yields the same value it would with no grouping at all, and that the edges registered are the ordinary ones. `parent_reads_child` is the direction most likely to be rejected by an over-eager scope check, and is also the one that sets up the cross-group hazard pinned in cross_group_teardown_hazard.json — legal to build, dangerous to outlive.

expect.note: a group's node reads a parent-owned node; the edge is an ordinary one
sibling groups are not isolated from each other
an ungrouped node may read a grouped one; grouping constrains lifetime, not reachability
propagation crosses group boundaries exactly as if no group existed
ending one group leaves the parent's and the sibling's nodes untouched
